content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95614

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive-Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
916) 322-5660                                322-5901                    322-6647                222-6444

September 21, 1977

77-09-204

Deborah E. McFarland
1507 Sacramento St.
Berkeley, CA 94702

Dear Ms. McFarland:

     Thank you for your letter of September 14, 1977
requesting informal advice about the mass mailing require-
ments of the Political Reform Act. In particular, you have
asked whether an invitation to a fund raiser sent on behalf
of a "clearly identified" state candidate must be forwarded
to the Fair Political Practices Commission pursuant to Gov-
ernment Code Section 84305.1/

     Section 84305 requires that a copy of every mass
mailing "in support of or in opposition to a state candidate"
be forwarded to the Commission on the same day that it is
mailed to the public generally.2/ The Commission has con-
cluded that the phrase "in support of ... a state candidate"
is synonymous with the concept of expressly advocating the
nomination or election of a clearly identified candidate, as
defined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(c). See Opinion
requested by Ralph Juvinall, et al., 2 FPPC Opinions 110,
114 (No. 75-018-A, et al., August 3, 1976).  Accordingly, a
mass mailing will be "in support of" a clearly identified
candidate, within the meaning of Section 84305, if it con-
tains express words of advocacy such as "vote for," "elect,"
"support," or if, taken as a whole, it unambiguously urges a
particular result in an election.  See 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18225(c)(2).

_____________
1/    All references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

2/    A mass mailing is any mailing of 200 or more
identical or nearly identical pieces of mail sent within a
calendar month.  See Government Code 82041.5; 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18241.

25

Deborah E. McFarland
September 21, 1977
Page Two

     After reviewing the "typical" invitations to fund
raisers that you forwarded to us, the staff has concluded
that, taken as a whole, such invitations do unambiguously
weigh a particular result in an election. An invitation to a
candidate's fund raiser evidences the sponsors' support of
the candidate who will benefit from the fund raiser and
necessarily involves a request by the sponsors that others
also support the candidate. Accordingly, we think that
"typical" invitations to fund raisers contain the type of
"support" contemplated by Section 8430.5 and, therefore, must
be forwarded to the Commission if the other requirements of
that provision are satisfied.

     If you have any further questions, please do not
hesitate to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Kenneth H. Pinney
                                        Staff Counsel

KHF:plh

1507 Sacramento Street
Berkeley, Calif. 94702

September 14, 1977


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, Calif. 95814

I am engaged in compiling state requirements with regard
to mass mailings. In this regard, I have been specifically asked about
requirements concerning invitations to fundraising events.

I believe that the requirements are clear as to when
identification of the sender identification is required. However, the requirement
that state candidates must send a copy to the Commission is more
questionably applicable. The "typical" invitations certainly address
themselves to "clearly identified candidates." However, whether or not
such invitations "clearly advocate the nomination, election or defeat"
or "a particular result in an election" when taken as a whole, is, at
best, a borderline decision. Moreover, the requirement's purpose
(deterring or opposing to public scrutiny dishonest or malicious allegations)
would not seem to be served when such an invitation is restricted to the
"typical" language of fundraising invitations. Therefore, I would
suggest that the second requirement of § 83405 should be inapplicable
to the "typical" invitation such as those attached.

I would very much appreciate receiving an informal opinion
addressing this question.

Thank you.

Sincerely,

Deborah E. McFarland

*While a "typical" invitation is perhaps an illusory concept
most invitations are restricted to information about the event is for,
when and where it will be held, contribution amount, notable participants,
supporters or individuals who will attend, etc. I have enclosed several
examples of invitations which I believe are "typical."
